publications.editedComment: One button, one route, keep the claim tight: the Google icon and label call `loginWithGoogle`, which logs the destination and sets `window.location.href` to `/api/auth/google`. That's the whole local beat. No token gets parsed, no popup gets opened, no account state gets written, and no success or failure comes back through this Component. OAuth begins only after the browser leaves for the server endpoint. The button is the verse; the auth route and callback carry the rest of the track.

Small form. Clear cargo. Title and icon name are required; label, description, and link may be left empty, while public visibility begins true. The record's type is fixed to `nav` both in the initial form and again before submission. `handleSubmit` refuses incomplete required fields, asks the Smartbar store to create the icon, and only after a successful result with an id adds that icon to the active Smartbar and routes to `/icons`. No local success banner or rollback lives here. Create first. Add second. Leave the harbor only when the new record has an id.

The future here is deterministic enough to be boring, which is a compliment. The form collects title, optional label and description, an icon identifier, optional link, and public visibility. Despite accepting a `SmartIcon` shape, it always forces `type` to `nav`; there is no type selector. Submission stops without title or icon, then calls `smartbarStore.createIcon`. Only a successful response containing an id leads to `addIconToSmartBar` and navigation to the icon gallery. A failed creation leaves the user on the page and receives no local message. I foresee confusion there—not because the save boundary is unclear, but because failure is silent in this Component.

Okay, this disaster is contained! The editor clones the incoming `icon` prop into a local form and reclones it whenever the prop changes, so typing does not mutate the parent record directly. Title, label, description, designer, icon name, link, type, and public status are editable, with the current icon rendered as a live preview. Footer controls can be hidden, and saving can be forbidden through props. `save` refuses when saving is disabled or the record lacks an id, then delegates to `smartbarStore.updateIcon`; success emits `close`. There is no local error display, delete action, or automatic parent refresh. Potential: high. Blast radius: pleasingly small.

Prop comes in, local copy gets spun; when the parent icon changes, the watcher makes a fresh one. The form previews `form.icon` with a help fallback and edits metadata, link, navigation type, utility type, and public state. `allowSave` can shut the write path down, while `showFooterActions` can remove both buttons from view. Save needs an id, calls `smartbarStore.updateIcon(id, form)`, and emits `close` only on success. Cancel emits the same close event without writing. No delete, no optimistic mutation, no error verse—just a bounded editor with the store on persistence duty.

This account has four locked icon ids removed before the gallery is shown. The remaining records may be filtered to all, the active user's own, public-only, navigation, or utility icons. Each card displays its type, graphic, label or title, optional description, and whether it currently belongs to the Smartbar. Add and remove actions delegate to the Smartbar store; a separate toggle enables or disables custom icons. Administrators may open an `edit-icon` modal, while every visitor may refresh and route to the add form. Initialization hydrates and refreshes the store on mount. The gallery manages membership and selection, not icon execution. That account is settled.

`filteredIcons` is the core pipeline: reject locked ids 1, 2, 3, and 8; optionally match the active user; optionally require public visibility; optionally match `nav` or `utility`. The rendered grid then derives Smartbar membership from `smartBarIds` and dispatches add or remove through store methods. Custom-icon enablement is another store call. Admins alone receive the edit modal; its selected record is local state and closes by clearing that reference. Mount calls `smartbarStore.initialize({ hydrate: true, refresh: true })`, while the refresh button can force another fetch. The Component is a stateful catalog adapter, not the source of icon records or route behavior.

One cup, one path. The Component is a router link to `/achievements` containing the Kind jellybean icon and a hover scale. Its only computed value reads `achievementStore.achievementCountForUser`, falls back to zero, and formats the exposed navigation label as `count /11`. That label is offered to the parent through `defineExpose`; it is not rendered locally. No achievements are fetched, awarded, or modified here, and no click handler competes with the router link. The store provides the count, the parent may provide the words, and this small leaf simply points toward the achievement room.

A fortunate little shortcut: the whole Component is a link to `/achievements`, wrapped around the jellybean icon. The Achievement store contributes one number, `achievementCountForUser`; absent data becomes zero, and the computed result is exposed to a parent as `${count} /11`. The label does not appear inside this template, so its usefulness depends on the surrounding Smartbar reading the exposed value. No achievement list loads, no reward is granted, and no custom navigation rule intervenes. Sometimes the happy accident is finding a Component whose complete contract fits in a few lines.
